Recent work uses low-rank recurrent neural networks (lrRNNs) to infer low-dimensional latent dynamics and connectivity structure from observed activity, enabling a mechanistic interpretation of the dynamics. However, standard approaches for training lrRNNs can recover spurious structures irrelevant to the underlying dynamics. We first characterize the identifiability of connectivity structures in lrRNNs and determine conditions under which a unique solution exists. Then, to find such solutions, we develop an inference framework based on maximum entropy and continuous normalizing flows (CNFs), trained via flow matching. Instead of estimating a single connectivity matrix, our method learns the maximally unbiased distribution over connection weights consistent with observed dynamics. This approach captures complex yet necessary distributions such as heavy-tailed connectivity found in empirical data. We validate our method on synthetic datasets with connectivity structures that generate multistable attractors, limit cycles, and ring attractors, and demonstrate its applicability in recordings from rat frontal cortex during decision-making. Our framework shifts circuit inference from recovering connectivity to identifying which connectivity structures are computationally required, and which are artifacts of underconstrained inference.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses challenges in inferring neural network connectivity from observed dynamics, using a novel framework based on maximum entropy and continuous normalizing flows to mitigate degenerate solutions.
AI.motivation: Inferring the connectivity structure of neural circuits from population activity recordings is challenging due to redundancies—multiple structures can produce the same dynamics.
AI.method: The authors developed a framework leveraging maximum entropy principles and continuous normalizing flows (CNFs), trained via flow matching, to infer unbiased distributions of connection weights instead of estimating a single matrix.
AI.result: The framework accurately captured connectivity structures in synthetic datasets with various dynamics (e.g., multistable attractors, ring attractors) and worked effectively on rat frontal cortex recordings during decision-making.
AI.conclusion: This method refines circuit inference by distinguishing between computationally required connectivity structures and artifacts of ambiguous inference, enhancing the interpretability of neural population dynamics.

title: Chasing Autonomy: Dynamic Retargeting and Control Guided RL for Performant and Controllable Humanoid Running
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Humanoid robots have the promise of locomoting like humans, including fast and dynamic running. Recently, reinforcement learning (RL) controllers that can mimic human motions have become popular as they can generate very dynamic behaviors, but they are often restricted to single motion play-back which hinders their deployment in long duration and autonomous locomotion. In this paper, we present a pipeline to dynamically retarget human motions through an optimization routine with hard constraints to generate improved periodic reference libraries from a single human demonstration. We then study the effect of both the reference motion and the reward structure on the reference and commanded velocity tracking, concluding that a goal-conditioned and control-guided reward which tracks dynamically optimized human data results in the best performance. We deploy the policy on hardware, demonstrating its speed and endurance by achieving running speeds of up to 3.3 m/s on a Unitree G1 robot and traversing hundreds of meters in real-world environments. Additionally, to demonstrate the controllability of the locomotion, we use the controller in a full perception and planning autonomy stack for obstacle avoidance while running outdoors.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a method to enhance humanoid robot running through dynamic human motion retargeting, resulting in fast, enduring, and autonomous locomotion.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to improve humanoid robot locomotion by addressing limitations of reinforcement learning controllers, which are restricted to replicating single human motions and remain unsuitable for prolonged and autonomous use.
AI.method: The authors created a pipeline that optimizes periodic motion reference libraries from single human demonstrations, combines this with a goal-conditioned and control-guided reward structure, and integrates it into robot controllers.
AI.result: The proposed system achieved running speeds up to 3.3 m/s on a Unitree G1 robot, traversed hundreds of meters, and demonstrated capability in real-world obstacle-avoidance scenarios as part of an autonomy stack.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic human motion optimization and a tailored reward structure significantly enhance humanoid robot running capabilities in speed, endurance, and autonomous obstacle handling.

title: IncreRTL: Traceability-Guided Incremental RTL Generation under Requirement Evolution
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in generating RTL code from natural-language descriptions, but existing methods remain static and struggle to adapt to evolving design requirements, potentially causing structural drift and costly full regeneration. We propose IncreRTL, a LLM-driven framework for incremental RTL generation under requirement evolution. By constructing requirement-code traceability links to locate and regenerate affected code segments, IncreRTL achieves accurate and consistent updates. Evaluated on our newly constructed EvoRTL-Bench, IncreRTL demonstrates notable improvements in regeneration consistency and efficiency, advancing LLM-based RTL generation toward practical engineering deployment.

AI.tldr: IncreRTL is a framework designed for adaptive RTL code generation using LLMs, enabling updates based on evolving design requirements.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in LLM-based RTL code generation methods that fail to adapt efficiently to changes in design requirements, leading to structural issues and inefficiencies.
AI.method: The authors developed 'IncreRTL,' a model that relies on requirement-code traceability links to isolate and regenerate only the affected code segments as requirements evolve.
AI.result: Evaluations on the newly constructed EvoRTL-Bench showed that IncreRTL improves both consistency and efficiency in RTL code regeneration.
AI.conclusion: IncreRTL advances the use of LLMs in real-world RTL code generation tasks by effectively managing requirement evolution, making such systems more practical for engineering use.

title: Doctorina MedBench: End-to-End Evaluation of Agent-Based Medical AI
summary:
We present Doctorina MedBench, a comprehensive evaluation framework for agent-based medical AI based on the simulation of realistic physician-patient interactions. Unlike traditional medical benchmarks that rely on solving standardized test questions, the proposed approach models a multi-step clinical dialogue in which either a physician or an AI system must collect medical history, analyze attached materials (including laboratory reports, images, and medical documents), formulate differential diagnoses, and provide personalized recommendations. System performance is evaluated using the D.O.T.S. metric, which consists of four components: Diagnosis, Observations/Investigations, Treatment, and Step Count, enabling assessment of both clinical correctness and dialogue efficiency.
  The system also incorporates a multi-level testing and quality monitoring architecture designed to detect model degradation during both development and deployment. The framework supports safety-oriented trap cases, category-based random sampling of clinical scenarios, and full regression testing. The dataset currently contains more than 1,000 clinical cases covering over 750 diagnoses. The universality of the evaluation metrics allows the framework to be used not only to assess medical AI systems, but also to evaluate physicians and support the development of clinical reasoning skills. Our results suggest that simulation of clinical dialogue may provide a more realistic assessment of clinical competence compared to traditional examination-style benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Doctorina MedBench is a framework designed to evaluate medical AI systems through simulated physician-patient interactions, focusing on diagnosis, personalized recommendations, and efficiency metrics.
AI.motivation: Traditional medical AI benchmarks rely on test questions, lacking realism in assessing clinical competence. The paper addresses this gap by proposing a simulation-based evaluation framework.
AI.method: The framework uses simulated multi-step clinical dialogues, graded with the D.O.T.S. metric (Diagnosis, Observations/Investigations, Treatment, and Step Count). It includes safety tests, random sampling of cases, and regression testing.
AI.result: Doctorina MedBench comprises 1,000 clinical cases spanning 750 diagnoses, measuring both accuracy and dialogue efficiency while averting model degradation.
AI.conclusion: Clinical dialogue simulation provides a more realistic and comprehensive evaluation of medical AI and physicians compared to traditional test-based frameworks.

title: On the Expressive Power of Contextual Relations in Transformers
summary: Transformer architectures have achieved remarkable empirical success in modeling contextual relationships in natural language, yet a precise mathematical characterization of their expressive power remains incomplete. In this work, we introduce a measure-theoretic framework for contextual representations in which texts are modeled as probability measures over a semantic embedding space, and contextual relations between words, are represented as coupling measures between them. Within this setting, we introduce Sinkhorn Transformer, a transformer-like architecture. Our main result is a universal approximation theorem: any continuous coupling function between probability measures, that encodes the semantic relation coupling measure, can be uniformly approximated by a Sinkhorn Transformer with appropriate parameters.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Sinkhorn Transformer which mathematically approximates contextual relationships in natural language using a measure-theoretic framework.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the gap in a precise mathematical characterization of transformers' ability to model contextual relationships in natural language processing.
AI.method: Texts are modeled as probability measures in a semantic embedding space, and the contextual relations between words are represented as coupling measures. The Sinkhorn Transformer is based on this framework and its approximation capabilities are established mathematically.
AI.result: The paper proves a universal approximation theorem, showing that continuous coupling functions between probability measures can be uniformly approximated by the Sinkhorn Transformer.
AI.conclusion: The Sinkhorn Transformer establishes the foundational result that it is capable of universal approximation for contextual relationships in textual data, advancing theoretical understanding of transformer architectures.

title: MAGNET: Autonomous Expert Model Generation via Decentralized Autoresearch and BitNet Training
summary: We present MAGNET (Model Autonomously Growing Network), a decentralized system for autonomous generation, training, and serving of domain-expert language models across commodity hardware. MAGNET integrates four components: (1) autoresearch, an autonomous ML research pipeline that automates dataset generation, hyperparameter exploration, evaluation, and error-driven iteration; (2) BitNet b1.58 ternary training, enabling CPU-native inference via bitnet.cpp without GPU hardware; (3) DiLoCo-based distributed merging for communication-efficient aggregation of domain specialists; and (4) on-chain contribution tracking on the HOOTi EVM chain. We validate autoresearch through three case studies: video safety classification (balanced accuracy 0.9287 to 0.9851), cryptocurrency directional prediction (41% to 54.9% hit rate), and BitNet hyperparameter optimization (10-phase sweep, -16.7% validation loss).

AI.tldr: MAGNET is a decentralized framework for creating and serving domain-specific language models using affordable hardware, emphasizing automation and collaborative improvement.
AI.motivation: To enable accessible advancements in language model training and serving by leveraging decentralized systems and commodity hardware.
AI.method: MAGNET comprises four core elements: an autonomous research pipeline (autoresearch), ternary training (BitNet b1.58) for CPU compatibility, distributed merging using DiLoCo, and blockchain-based contribution tracking.
AI.result: Demonstrated effectiveness across three domains: improved video safety classification accuracy to 0.9851, cryptocurrency prediction hit rate to 54.9%, and reduced validation loss in BitNet optimization through iterative sweeps.
AI.conclusion: MAGNET successfully showcases decentralized and autonomous approaches for domain-specific language model development, achieving impressive results on affordable infrastructure.

title: Evaluating Synthetic Images as Effective Substitutes for Experimental Data in Surface Roughness Classification
summary: Hard coatings play a critical role in industry, with ceramic materials offering outstanding hardness and thermal stability for applications that demand superior mechanical performance. However, deploying artificial intelligence (AI) for surface roughness classification is often constrained by the need for large labeled datasets and costly high-resolution imaging equipment. In this study, we explore the use of synthetic images, generated with Stable Diffusion XL, as an efficient alternative or supplement to experimentally acquired data for classifying ceramic surface roughness. We show that augmenting authentic datasets with generative images yields test accuracies comparable to those obtained using exclusively experimental images, demonstrating that synthetic images effectively reproduce the structural features necessary for classification. We further assess method robustness by systematically varying key training hyperparameters (epoch count, batch size, and learning rate), and identify configurations that preserve performance while reducing data requirements. Our results indicate that generative AI can substantially improve data efficiency and reliability in materials-image classification workflows, offering a practical route to lower experimental cost, accelerate model development, and expand AI applicability in materials engineering.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates using generative AI (Stable Diffusion XL) for creating synthetic images to classify ceramic surface roughness, achieving performance comparable to experimental data while reducing data and cost requirements.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the challenges in deploying AI for surface roughness classification, particularly the high cost and large labeled dataset requirements associated with experimental imaging methods.
AI.method: Synthetic images were generated using Stable Diffusion XL, and these were augmented with experimental data to train classification models. Key hyperparameters were adjusted to optimize performance and robustness.
AI.result: Augmenting datasets with synthetic images resulted in test accuracies on par with using only experimental images. The approach reduced data requirements while maintaining classification reliability.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI, such as Stable Diffusion XL, can enhance data efficiency, lower imaging costs, and accelerate AI adoption in materials engineering, making it a valuable supplement to traditional experimental methods.

title: Per-Bank Memory Bandwidth Regulation for Predictable and Performant Real-Time System
summary:
Modern multicore system-on-chips (SoCs) share off-chip DRAM across cores, where bank-level interference can significantly degrade performance and threaten real-time guarantees. While prior work has focused on per-core bandwidth regulation, these approaches treat main memory as a monolithic resource and overlook DRAM's inherent bank-level parallelism.
  We show that DRAM interference is fundamentally a bank-level phenomenon. We characterize the guaranteed bandwidth of modern DRAM, demonstrate that it remains effectively constant across generations, and show how this limitation can be exploited by single-bank attacks. These results highlight the need for bank-aware memory management for predictable and efficient real-time systems.
  We design and implement a novel per-bank memory bandwidth regulator in an open-source RISC-V SoC and evaluate it using FireSim with both synthetic and real-world workloads. Our evaluation demonstrates that per-bank regulation effectively mitigates adversarial bank contention and achieves a 5.74x average throughput improvement for best-effort workloads over traditional bank-oblivious approaches while providing the same-level of performance isolation guarantees for real-time workloads.

AI.tldr: This study reveals that DRAM interference is a bank-level issue, and proposes a per-bank memory bandwidth regulator for real-time systems.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address performance degradation and real-time guarantee threats caused by DRAM interference, leveraging DRAM's bank-level parallelism, which is overlooked in traditional bandwidth regulation methods.
AI.method: The authors design and implement a novel per-bank memory bandwidth regulator on an open-source RISC-V SoC, evaluating its effectiveness using both synthetic and real-world workloads in FireSim.
AI.result: The per-bank regulation approach significantly mitigates adversarial bank contention, achieving a 5.74x average throughput improvement for best-effort workloads and maintaining performance isolation for real-time tasks.
AI.conclusion: Bank-aware memory management is critical for enhancing both predictability and efficiency in modern real-time systems, surpassing traditional bank-oblivious approaches.

title: GUIDE: Resolving Domain Bias in GUI Agents through Real-Time Web Video Retrieval and Plug-and-Play Annotation
summary: Large vision-language models have endowed GUI agents with strong general capabilities for interface understanding and interaction. However, due to insufficient exposure to domain-specific software operation data during training, these agents exhibit significant domain bias - they lack familiarity with the specific operation workflows (planning) and UI element layouts (grounding) of particular applications, limiting their real-world task performance. In this paper, we present GUIDE (GUI Unbiasing via Instructional-Video Driven Expertise), a training-free, plug-and-play framework that resolves GUI agent domain bias by autonomously acquiring domain-specific expertise from web tutorial videos through a retrieval-augmented automated annotation pipeline. GUIDE introduces two key innovations. First, a subtitle-driven Video-RAG pipeline unlocks video semantics through subtitle analysis, performing progressive three-stage retrieval - domain classification, topic extraction, and relevance matching - to identify task-relevant tutorial videos. Second, a fully automated annotation pipeline built on an inverse dynamics paradigm feeds consecutive keyframes enhanced with UI element detection into VLMs, inferring the required planning and grounding knowledge that are injected into the agent's corresponding modules to address both manifestations of domain bias. Extensive experiments on OSWorld demonstrate GUIDE's generality as a plug-and-play component for both multi-agent systems and single-model agents. It consistently yields over 5% improvements and reduces execution steps - without modifying any model parameters or architecture - validating GUIDE as an architecture-agnostic enhancement to bridge GUI agent domain bias.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces GUIDE, a framework to eliminate domain bias in GUI agents using tutorial videos and automated annotation.
AI.motivation: GUI agents struggle with domain bias due to inadequate exposure to specific UI workflows and elements during training, reducing their real-world effectiveness.
AI.method: GUIDE autonomously acquires domain-specific expertise by leveraging a retrieval-augmented automated annotation pipeline, utilizing progressive retrieval of relevant tutorial videos and inverse dynamics-based annotations.
AI.result: Experiments show GUIDE improves GUI agent performance by over 5% and reduces execution steps without altering their architecture or parameters.
AI.conclusion: GUIDE enhances GUI agent capabilities by resolving domain bias in a training-free, architecture-agnostic, and plug-and-play manner.

title: Passivity-Based Control of Electrographic Seizures in a Neural Mass Model of Epilepsy
summary: Recent advances in neurotechnologies and decades of scientific and clinical research have made closed-loop electrical neuromodulation one of the most promising avenues for the treatment of drug-resistant epilepsy (DRE), a condition that affects over 15 million individuals globally. Yet, with the existing clinical state of the art, only 18% of patients with DRE who undergo closed-loop neuromodulation become seizure-free. In a recent study, we demonstrated that a simple proportional feedback policy based on the framework of passivity-based control (PBC) can significantly outperform the clinical state of the art. However, this study was purely numerical and lacked rigorous mathematical analysis. The present study addresses this gap and provides the first rigorous analysis of PBC for the closed-loop control of epileptic seizures. Using the celebrated Epileptor neural mass model of epilepsy, we analytically demonstrate that (i) seizure dynamics are, in their standard form, neither passive nor passivatable, (ii) epileptic dynamics, despite their lack of passivity, can be stabilized by sufficiently strong passive feedback, and (iii) seizure dynamics can be passivated via proper output redesign. To our knowledge, our results provide the first rigorous passivity-based analysis of epileptic seizure dynamics, as well as a theoretically-grounded framework for sensor placement and feedback design for a new form of closed-loop neuromodulation with the potential to transform seizure management in DRE.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a rigorous mathematical analysis of passivity-based control (PBC) for managing drug-resistant epilepsy (DRE) using closed-loop neuromodulation.
AI.motivation: Only 18% of patients with DRE undergoing closed-loop neuromodulation achieve seizure freedom, necessitating a more effective control strategy.
AI.method: The study uses the Epileptor neural mass model to analytically investigate seizure dynamics and evaluate the feasibility of PBC for stabilizing these dynamics.
AI.result: The authors demonstrate that epileptic dynamics can be stabilized by sufficiently strong passive feedback and provide a framework for output redesign to improve passivity and seizure control.
AI.conclusion: The findings establish a robust, theoretically-grounded framework for enhancing closed-loop neuromodulation strategies for DRE, potentially improving treatment outcomes.

title: Emergent Neural Automaton Policies: Learning Symbolic Structure from Visuomotor Trajectories
summary: Scaling robot learning to long-horizon tasks remains a formidable challenge. While end-to-end policies often lack the structural priors needed for effective long-term reasoning, traditional neuro-symbolic methods rely heavily on hand-crafted symbolic priors. To address the issue, we introduce ENAP (Emergent Neural Automaton Policy), a framework that allows a bi-level neuro-symbolic policy adaptively emerge from visuomotor demonstrations. Specifically, we first employ adaptive clustering and an extension of the L* algorithm to infer a Mealy state machine from visuomotor data, which serves as an interpretable high-level planner capturing latent task modes. Then, this discrete structure guides a low-level reactive residual network to learn precise continuous control via behavior cloning (BC). By explicitly modeling the task structure with discrete transitions and continuous residuals, ENAP achieves high sample efficiency and interpretability without requiring task-specific labels. Extensive experiments on complex manipulation and long-horizon tasks demonstrate that ENAP outperforms state-of-the-art (SoTA) end-to-end VLA policies by up to 27% in low-data regimes, while offering a structured representation of robotic intent (Fig. 1).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ENAP, a bi-level neuro-symbolic policy approach for robot learning. ENAP learns an interpretable high-level planner and low-level controller from visuomotor demonstrations for effective long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome the limitations of both end-to-end policies (insufficient structural priors for long-term reasoning) and traditional neuro-symbolic methods (over-reliance on hand-crafted priors) in robot learning for long-horizon tasks.
AI.method: ENAP uses adaptive clustering and an extended L* algorithm to infer a Mealy state machine serving as a high-level planner. This machine guides a low-level reactive residual network trained via behavior cloning for precise control. It combines discrete structures with continuous residuals to achieve high efficiency and interpretability.
AI.result: ENAP outperforms state-of-the-art end-to-end visuomotor learning algorithms by up to 27% in low-data regimes and provides a structured, interpretable representation of robotic intent in complex manipulation and long-horizon tasks.
AI.conclusion: ENAP offers an effective, efficient, and interpretable framework for robotic learning, addressing structural and data-efficiency challenges in long-horizon tasks.

title: ReCUBE: Evaluating Repository-Level Context Utilization in Code Generation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently emerged as capable coding assistants that operate over large codebases through either agentic exploration or full-context generation. Existing benchmarks capture a broad range of coding capabilities, such as resolving GitHub issues, but none of them directly isolate and measure how effectively LLMs leverage repository-level context during code generation. To address this, we introduce ReCUBE, a benchmark in which LLMs reconstruct a masked file within a real-world repository, using all remaining source files, dependency specifications, and documentation as their only source of context. ReCUBE evaluates reconstructed code with usage-aware test cases that simulate both internal module logic and external cross-file integration, reflecting real-world software usage patterns. We further propose the Caller-Centric Exploration (CCE) toolkit, a set of dependency graph-based tools that can be integrated into agentic frameworks to guide agents toward the most relevant caller files during repository exploration. Experiments across eight models in four settings show that repository-level context utilization remains highly challenging even for state-of-the-art models, with GPT-5 achieving only 37.57% strict pass rate in the full-context setting. Agents augmented with our CCE toolkit consistently outperform all baselines across all evaluated models, with improvements of up to 7.56% in strict pass rate. We release our benchmark, code, and evaluation framework as open source for the NLP research community.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ReCUBE, a benchmark for evaluating how well LLMs utilize repository-level context to reconstruct masked files in software projects. It also proposes the Caller-Centric Exploration (CCE) toolkit to enhance agentic model performance.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks do not adequately measure how effectively LLMs use repository-level context during code generation, a critical skill for addressing real-world software challenges.
AI.method: The authors develop ReCUBE, a tool where LLMs reconstruct masked files based on remaining source code, documentation, and dependency details. They also propose the CCE toolkit to guide agentic models through dependency graph exploration, enhancing focus on relevant caller files.
AI.result: Experiments across eight models demonstrated that state-of-the-art models struggle with repository-level context, with GPT-5 achieving only 37.57% pass rate. Models integrated with CCE tools consistently showed performance gains, with up to 7.56% improvements in strict pass rates.
AI.conclusion: Despite ReCUBE highlighting significant challenges in repository-level context comprehension, tools like the CCE toolkit offer a pathway for meaningful improvements. The benchmark, tools, and evaluation framework are open-sourced to advance research in this domain.

title: Gradient-Informed Training for Low-Resource Multilingual Speech Translation
summary: In low-resource multilingual speech-to-text translation, uniform architectural sharing across languages frequently introduces representation conflicts that impede convergence. This work proposes a principled methodology to automatically determine layer-specific sharing patterns by mining training gradient information. Our approach employs three distinct analysis strategies: distance-based language clustering, self/cross-task divergence metrics for capacity allocation, and joint factorization coupled with canonical correlation analysis for subspace alignment. Extensive evaluation across four language pairs (using the SeamlessM4T-Medium architecture) demonstrates persistent improvements in translation quality metrics.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses representation conflicts in multilingual speech-to-text translation by introducing a method to determine layer-specific sharing patterns.
AI.motivation: Multilingual speech-to-text translation faces convergence issues due to uniform representation sharing across languages.
AI.method: The approach utilizes gradient information analysis, language clustering, divergence metrics, and subspace alignment techniques.
AI.result: The proposed methodology improves translation quality metrics across four language pairs using SeamlessM4T-Medium architecture.
AI.conclusion: Mining gradient information provides effective layer-specific sharing patterns, resolving conflicts and enhancing multilingual translation.

title: Asymptotic Optimism for Tensor Regression Models with Applications to Neural Network Compression
summary: We study rank selection for low-rank tensor regression under random covariates design. Under a Gaussian random-design model and some mild conditions, we derive population expressions for the expected training-testing discrepancy (optimism) for both CP and Tucker decomposition. We further demonstrate that the optimism is minimized at the true tensor rank for both CP and Tucker regression. This yields a prediction-oriented rank-selection rule that aligns with cross-validation and extends naturally to tensor-model averaging. We also discuss conditions under which under- or over-ranked models may appear preferable, thereby clarifying the scope of the method. Finally, we showcase its practical utility on a real-world image regression task and extend its application to tensor-based compression of neural network, highlighting its potential for model selection in deep learning.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a method for selecting tensor rank in regression models using population expressions for expected training-testing discrepancy. The approach is validated on image regression and neural network compression.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of selecting an appropriate tensor rank for low-rank tensor regression, which is critical for accurate predictions and efficient model implementations.
AI.method: The authors analyze the expected training-testing discrepancy under Gaussian random-design conditions for CP and Tucker decompositions, proposing a rank-selection rule based on minimizing this discrepancy.
AI.result: They find that the proposed rank-selection rule aligns with cross-validation, identifies true tensor ranks, and has practical benefits for image regression and neural network tensor compression.
AI.conclusion: The findings emphasize the utility of this prediction-oriented rank-selection method, underscoring its role in accurate modeling and its adaptability for deep learning tasks.

title: A Compression Perspective on Simplicity Bias
summary: Deep neural networks exhibit a simplicity bias, a well-documented tendency to favor simple functions over complex ones. In this work, we cast new light on this phenomenon through the lens of the Minimum Description Length principle, formalizing supervised learning as a problem of optimal two-part lossless compression. Our theory explains how simplicity bias governs feature selection in neural networks through a fundamental trade-off between model complexity (the cost of describing the hypothesis) and predictive power (the cost of describing the data). Our framework predicts that as the amount of available training data increases, learners transition through qualitatively different features -- from simple spurious shortcuts to complex features -- only when the reduction in data encoding cost justifies the increased model complexity. Consequently, we identify distinct data regimes where increasing data promotes robustness by ruling out trivial shortcuts, and conversely, regimes where limiting data can act as a form of complexity-based regularization, preventing the learning of unreliable complex environmental cues. We validate our theory on a semi-synthetic benchmark showing that the feature selection of neural networks follows the same trajectory of solutions as optimal two-part compressors.

AI.tldr: Deep neural networks tend to prefer simpler functions, a phenomenon explained using Minimum Description Length principle in this paper. It links simplicity bias to a trade-off between model complexity and predictive power in feature selection. Results demonstrate data regimes, with increasing data aiding robustness, while limited data helps prevent overfitting to complex cues.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to understand the simplicity bias in deep neural networks and explain its effects in the context of feature selection during supervised learning.
AI.method: The authors utilize the Minimum Description Length principle to formalize supervised learning as lossless compression, analyzing the trade-off between model complexity and data encoding costs. They test their framework with semi-synthetic benchmarks.
AI.result: Findings show neural network feature selection aligns with the solutions of optimal two-part compressors. The transition from simple to complex features depends on data availability and encoding cost justifications. Distinct data regimes fostering robustness or acting as regularization are identified.
AI.conclusion: This study provides theoretical insights into simplicity bias in neural networks and demonstrates its implications for feature selection, data-driven model robustness, and regularization strategies based on data scarcity.

title: Focus-to-Perceive Representation Learning: A Cognition-Inspired Hierarchical Framework for Endoscopic Video Analysis
summary: Endoscopic video analysis is essential for early gastrointestinal screening but remains hindered by limited high-quality annotations. While self-supervised video pre-training shows promise, existing methods developed for natural videos prioritize dense spatio-temporal modeling and exhibit motion bias, overlooking the static, structured semantics critical to clinical decision-making. To address this challenge, we propose Focus-to-Perceive Representation Learning (FPRL), a cognition-inspired hierarchical framework that emulates clinical examination. FPRL first focuses on intra-frame lesion-centric regions to learn static semantics, and then perceives their evolution across frames to model contextual semantics. To achieve this, FPRL employs a hierarchical semantic modeling mechanism that explicitly distinguishes and collaboratively learns both types of semantics. Specifically, it begins by capturing static semantics via teacher-prior adaptive masking (TPAM) combined with multi-view sparse sampling. This approach mitigates redundant temporal dependencies and enables the model to concentrate on lesion-related local semantics. Following this, contextual semantics are derived through cross-view masked feature completion (CVMFC) and attention-guided temporal prediction (AGTP). These processes establish cross-view correspondences and effectively model structured inter-frame evolution, thereby reinforcing temporal semantic continuity while preserving global contextual integrity. Extensive experiments on 11 endoscopic video datasets show that FPRL achieves superior performance across diverse downstream tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness in endoscopic video representation learning. The code is available at https://github.com/MLMIP/FPRL.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel self-supervised hierarchical framework called Focus-to-Perceive Representation Learning (FPRL) for endoscopic video analysis, which focuses on extracting lesion-centric features and modeling their contextual evolution.
AI.motivation: Current endoscopic video analysis is hampered by limited high-quality annotations and the inadequacy of existing self-supervised video pre-training methods to prioritize static and structured semantics crucial for clinical decision-making.
AI.method: The proposed FPRL framework employs a two-step hierarchical semantic modeling mechanism: (1) capturing static semantics using teacher-prior adaptive masking (TPAM) with multi-view sparse sampling, and (2) modeling contextual semantics through cross-view masked feature completion (CVMFC) and attention-guided temporal prediction (AGTP).
AI.result: Extensive experiments across 11 endoscopic video datasets reveal that FPRL outperforms other methods in various downstream tasks, showing its superior capability in learning endoscopic video representations.
AI.conclusion: FPRL offers a clinical examination-inspired solution for endoscopic video analysis by effectively modeling both static and contextual semantics, overcoming the limitations of existing methods and demonstrating significant potential in gastrointestinal screening applications.

title: Distributed Quantum Discrete Logarithm Algorithm
summary: Solving the discrete logarithm problem (DLP) with quantum computers is a fundamental task with important implications. Beyond Shor's algorithm, many researchers have proposed alternative solutions in recent years. However, due to current hardware limitations, the scale of DLP instances that can be addressed by quantum computers remains insufficient. To overcome this limitation, we propose a distributed quantum discrete logarithm algorithm that reduces the required quantum register size for solving DLPs. Specifically, we design a distributed quantum algorithm to determine whether the solution is contained in a given set. Based on this procedure, our method solves DLPs by identifying the intersection of sets containing the solution. Compared with Shor's original algorithm, our approach reduces the register size and can improve the success probability, while requiring no quantum communication.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a distributed quantum algorithm for solving the discrete logarithm problem (DLP) by requiring smaller quantum registers and no quantum communication.
AI.motivation: Current quantum computers face hardware limitations in addressing large-scale DLP instances, prompting the need for alternative methods with reduced resource requirements.
AI.method: The authors propose a distributed quantum algorithm that identifies the solution by finding intersections of sets, thereby reducing register size and eliminating the need for quantum communication.
AI.result: The proposed method reduces the quantum register size and enhances success probability compared to Shor's algorithm.
AI.conclusion: The distributed quantum algorithm provides a more resource-efficient alternative to solve DLPs, making them more feasible under current quantum hardware constraints.

title: IBEX: Internal Bandwidth-Efficient Compression Architecture for Scalable CXL Memory Expansion
comment: This paper has been accepted to ICS`26
summary: As the memory channel count is confined by physical dimensions, memory expanders appear to be a promising approach to extending memory capacity and channels by augmenting the existing I/O interface (e.g., PCIe) with memory-semantic protocols like CXL. Unfortunately, the physical constraints of a computing system restrict scalable capacity expansion with memory expanders. In this work, we propose a block-level compression scheme for modern memory expanders, IBEX, to achieve larger effective memory capacity. Given the performance overhead associated with block-level compression algorithms (e.g., LZ77), IBEX employs a promotion-based approach: only cold data is compressed, whereas hot data remains uncompressed. Our key innovation is internal bandwidth-efficient block management that precisely identifies cold pages with minimal metadata access overhead. Still, the promotion-based approach poses several performance-related challenges at the design level. Therefore, we also propose a shadowed promotion scheme that temporarily postpones the deallocation of promoted data, thereby mitigating the performance penalty incurred by demotion (i.e., recompression). Furthermore, we optimize our compression scheme by compacting metadata and co-locating multiple target blocks for efficient bandwidth utilization. Consequently, IBEX achieves an average of 1.28x-1.40x speedups compared to the state-of-the-art promotion-based block-level approaches. We open-source IBEX at https://github.com/relacslab/ibex-ics26.

AI.tldr: IBEX introduces a compression scheme for memory expanders, targeting increased memory capacity without excessive performance penalties.
AI.motivation: To overcome physical constraints in memory channel expansion and enhance memory capacity via a compression scheme for memory expanders.
AI.method: Promotion-based block-level compression where cold data is compressed and hot data remains uncompressed, supplemented by internal bandwidth-efficient block management and shadowed promotion.
AI.result: IBEX achieves 1.28x-1.40x speedups compared to existing methods, optimizing metadata and bandwidth utilization.
AI.conclusion: IBEX demonstrates an innovative approach to scalable memory capacity expansion through intelligent compression, balancing performance and overhead, and is open-sourced for further development.

title: AIRA_2: Overcoming Bottlenecks in AI Research Agents
summary: Existing research has identified three structural performance bottlenecks in AI research agents: (1) synchronous single-GPU execution constrains sample throughput, limiting the benefit of search; (2) a generalization gap where validation-based selection causes performance to degrade over extended search horizons; and (3) the limited capability of fixed, single-turn LLM operators imposes a ceiling on search performance. We introduce AIRA$_2$, which addresses these bottlenecks through three architectural choices: an asynchronous multi-GPU worker pool that increases experiment throughput linearly; a Hidden Consistent Evaluation protocol that delivers a reliable evaluation signal; and ReAct agents that dynamically scope their actions and debug interactively. On MLE-bench-30, AIRA$_2$ achieves a mean Percentile Rank of 71.8% at 24 hours - surpassing the previous best of 69.9% - and steadily improves to 76.0% at 72 hours. Ablation studies reveal that each component is necessary and that the "overfitting" reported in prior work was driven by evaluation noise rather than true data memorization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AIRA$_2$, addressing three key bottlenecks in AI research agents: sample throughput limitations, generalization gaps, and fixed operator restrictions. It features asynchronous multi-GPU execution, Hidden Consistent Evaluation, and dynamic ReAct agents.
AI.motivation: To solve bottlenecks in AI research concerning throughput, generalization, and performance in long-term horizons.
AI.method: Architectural improvements with asynchronous multi-GPU worker pool, reliable evaluation protocols, and dynamic action-scoping agents.
AI.result: AIRA$_2$ achieves state-of-the-art performance on MLE-bench-30, with a mean Percentile Rank of 71.8% at 24 hours and 76.0% at 72 hours.
AI.conclusion: The proposed solutions successfully address the identified performance bottlenecks, enabling scalable and effective AI research workflows.

title: Revisiting claims of extracranial biophoton detection from the human brain
summary: Ultraweak photon emission is the spontaneous emission of extremely low levels of light from a broad range of biological systems. Recent studies have reported that UPE measured extracranially can serve as a potential non-invasive biomarker of brain activity. Here, we show that this interpretation suffers from serious problems. First, when observed under properly dark conditions, the UPE from the head is much weaker than what is reported in certain papers on 'brain UPE' from human heads. Signals detected in these studies are overwhelmingly dominated by background light. Second, photons at wavelengths < 600 nm are strongly attenuated by scalp and skull tissues, and longer wavelengths fall largely outside the effective spectral sensitivity of the photomultiplier tubes (PMTs) used. As a consequence, even if UPE from the head is detected under properly background-free conditions, it is likely to be dominated by emission from the scalp rather than from the brain, certainly as long as PMTs are used. Our results emphasize the importance of careful experimental design to make genuine progress on this important question.

AI.tldr: This paper critiques the interpretation of ultraweak photon emission (UPE) as a non-invasive biomarker of brain activity, arguing that existing studies are flawed due to background light interference and tissue attenuation issues.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to challenge and refine the validity of UPE from the human head as a biomarker of brain activity, ensuring scientific accuracy and experimental reliability.
AI.method: The paper critically evaluates existing studies, analyzes the intensity of UPE under dark conditions, and examines how scalp and skull tissues attenuate signal wavelengths.
AI.result: Findings reveal that previously reported UPE intensities are likely dominated by background light and that tissue attenuation limits the ability to detect brain-related emissions using current technology.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights serious issues in prior interpretations and stresses the need for better experimental designs to accurately study UPE as a potential biomarker of brain activity.

title: Can Vision Foundation Models Navigate? Zero-Shot Real-World Evaluation and Lessons Learned
summary: Visual Navigation Models (VNMs) promise generalizable, robot navigation by learning from large-scale visual demonstrations. Despite growing real-world deployment, existing evaluations rely almost exclusively on success rate, whether the robot reaches its goal, which conceals trajectory quality, collision behavior, and robustness to environmental change. We present a real-world evaluation of five state-of-the-art VNMs (GNM, ViNT, NoMaD, NaviBridger, and CrossFormer) across two robot platforms and five environments spanning indoor and outdoor settings. Beyond success rate, we combine path-based metrics with vision-based goal-recognition scores and assess robustness through controlled image perturbations (motion blur, sunflare). Our analysis uncovers three systematic limitations: (a) even architecturally sophisticated diffusion and transformer-based models exhibit frequent collisions, indicating limited geometric understanding; (b) models fail to discriminate between different locations that are perceptually similar, however some semantics differences are present, causing goal prediction errors in repetitive environments; and (c) performance degrades under distribution shift. We will publicly release our evaluation codebase and dataset to facilitate reproducible benchmarking of VNMs.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates five state-of-the-art Visual Navigation Models (VNMs) across real-world settings, analyzing their path quality, collision behavior, and robustness to environmental changes. It identifies limitations in geometric understanding, location discrimination, and performance under shifts.
AI.motivation: While existing studies on VNMs focus on success rate as their main metric, this is insufficient for capturing critical aspects such as trajectory quality, collision avoidance, and adaptability to environmental variations. A deeper and more nuanced evaluation is necessary to understand VNMs' practical capabilities.
AI.method: The study conducts a real-world evaluation of five VNMs (GNM, ViNT, NoMaD, NaviBridger, and CrossFormer) across two robot platforms in five different environments (indoor and outdoor). Metrics extend beyond success rate to include path quality, vision-based goal-recognition scores, and controlled robustness tests with image perturbations (e.g., motion blur and sunflare).
AI.result: The analysis reveals three major limitations: frequent collisions due to lacking geometric understanding, failure to differentiate perceptually similar locations in repetitive environments, and reduced performance under distribution shifts like environmental changes.
AI.conclusion: VNMs, despite architectural sophistication, exhibit critical weaknesses in geometry comprehension, location discrimination, and handling distribution shifts. The study's findings underline these gaps and the need for better benchmarks; its dataset and codebase will be made publicly available to support further research.

title: The Specification as Quality Gate: Three Hypotheses on AI-Assisted Code Review
comment: 10 pages. Experiments, corpus, and specifications publicly available at https://github.com/czietsman/nuphirho.dev/tree/main/experiments
summary:
The dominant industry response to AI-generated code quality problems is to deploy AI reviewers. This paper argues that this response is structurally circular when executable specifications are absent: without an external reference, both the generating agent and the reviewing agent reason from the same artefact, share the same training distribution, and exhibit correlated failures. The review checks code against itself, not against intent.
  Three hypotheses are developed. First, that correlated errors in homogeneous LLM pipelines echo rather than cancel, a claim supported by convergent empirical evidence from multiple 2025-2026 studies and by three small contrived experiments reported here. The first two experiments are same-family (Claude reviewing Claude-generated code); the third extends to a cross-family panel of four models from three families. All use a planted bug corpus rather than a natural defect sample; they are directional evidence, not a controlled demonstration. Second, that executable specifications perform a domain transition in the Cynefin sense, converting enabling constraints into governing constraints and moving the problem from the complex domain to the complicated domain, a transition that AI makes economically viable at scale. Third, that the defect classes lying outside the reach of executable specifications form a well-defined residual, which is the legitimate and bounded target for AI review.
  The combined argument implies an architecture: specifications first, deterministic verification pipeline second, AI review only for the structural and architectural residual. This is not a claim that AI review is valueless. It is a claim about what it is actually for, and about what happens when it is deployed without the foundation that makes it non-circular.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques AI-driven code quality reviews due to their inherent circularity and proposes a solution based on executable specifications.
AI.motivation: AI-generated code often faces quality issues, and deploying AI reviewers is a common strategy. However, this practice encounters fundamental limitations, particularly when executable specifications are missing, leading to correlated failures.
AI.method: The study presents three hypotheses supported by both prior empirical findings (2025-2026 studies) and three experiments using planted bug corpora. The experiments examine error correlation in same-family and cross-family AI pipeline reviews.
AI.result: Empirical evidence shows that homogeneous AI pipelines amplify errors rather than reduce them. Executable specifications effectively mitigate this by transitioning quality assurance to a more structured domain, while residual defect classes that lie outside their scope can still benefit from AI reviews.
AI.conclusion: AI reviews should complement, not replace, deterministic systems underpinned by executable specifications. Without such foundations, AI reviewing methods lose their effectiveness and become structurally circular.

title: Methods for Knowledge Graph Construction from Text Collections: Development and Applications
summary: Virtually every sector of society is experiencing a dramatic growth in the volume of unstructured textual data that is generated and published, from news and social media online interactions, through open access scholarly communications and observational data in the form of digital health records and online drug reviews. The volume and variety of data across all this range of domains has created both unprecedented opportunities and pressing challenges for extracting actionable knowledge for several application scenarios. However, the extraction of rich semantic knowledge demands the deployment of scalable and flexible automatic methods adaptable across text genres and schema specifications. Moreover, the full potential of these data can only be unlocked by coupling information extraction methods with Semantic Web techniques for the construction of full-fledged Knowledge Graphs, that are semantically transparent, explainable by design and interoperable. In this thesis, we experiment with the application of Natural Language Processing, Machine Learning and Generative AI methods, powered by Semantic Web best practices, to the automatic construction of Knowledge Graphs from large text corpora, in three use case applications: the analysis of the Digital Transformation discourse in the global news and social media platforms; the mapping and trend analysis of recent research in the Architecture, Engineering, Construction and Operations domain from a large corpus of publications; the generation of causal relation graphs of biomedical entities from electronic health records and patient-authored drug reviews. The contributions of this thesis to the research community are in terms of benchmark evaluation results, the design of customized algorithms and the creation of data resources in the form of Knowledge Graphs, together with data analysis results built on top of them.

AI.tldr: This paper focuses on using NLP, Machine Learning, and Generative AI with Semantic Web techniques to create knowledge graphs across three domains: digital transformation discourse, AEC publications, and biomedical causal graphs.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to address the challenges posed by massive volumes of unstructured textual data in various domains, emphasizing the need for scalable methods to extract semantic knowledge and construct explainable, interoperable knowledge graphs.
AI.method: The authors apply a combination of Natural Language Processing, Machine Learning, Generative AI methods, and Semantic Web best practices to extract information and generate Knowledge Graphs from large text corpora.
AI.result: The paper provides benchmark evaluation results, introduces customized algorithms, and creates domain-specific knowledge graphs and analytical insights for three use cases: social media/news analysis, AEC domain publications, and biomedical entity relationships.
AI.conclusion: The research contributes to advancing data extraction methods, knowledge graph generation techniques, and data analysis frameworks, offering essential benchmarks, algorithms, and resources for future work.

title: Privacy-Accuracy Trade-offs in High-Dimensional LASSO under Perturbation Mechanisms
summary: We study privacy-preserving sparse linear regression in the high-dimensional regime, focusing on the LASSO estimator. We analyze two widely used mechanisms for differential privacy: output perturbation, which injects noise into the estimator, and objective perturbation, which adds a random linear term to the loss function. Using approximate message passing (AMP), we characterize the typical behavior of these estimators under random design and privacy noise. To quantify privacy, we adopt typical-case measures, including the on-average KL divergence, which admits a hypothesis-testing interpretation in terms of distinguishability between neighboring datasets. Our analysis reveals that sparsity plays a central role in shaping the privacy-accuracy trade-off: stronger regularization can improve privacy by stabilizing the estimator against single-point data changes. We further show that the two mechanisms exhibit qualitatively different behaviors. In particular, for objective perturbation, increasing the noise level can have non-monotonic effects, and excessive noise may destabilize the estimator, leading to increased sensitivity to data perturbations. Our results demonstrate that AMP provides a powerful framework for analyzing privacy-accuracy trade-offs in high-dimensional sparse models.

AI.tldr: This study investigates privacy-preserving sparse linear regression, specifically analyzing the LASSO estimator under two differential privacy mechanisms—output and objective perturbation—using approximate message passing (AMP).
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand how sparsity influences privacy-accuracy trade-offs in high-dimensional sparse models and to analyze typical behaviors of noise-affected estimators using more realistic privacy metrics.
AI.method: The researchers used approximate message passing (AMP) to model and characterize estimators' behaviors, adopting privacy measures like on-average KL divergence to evaluate privacy-accuracy trade-offs.
AI.result: The analysis shows that sparsity greatly affects the privacy-accuracy trade-off, with regularization often improving privacy by stabilizing estimators. They also found differing behaviors between perturbation mechanisms, such as non-monotonic effects of noise in objective perturbation.
AI.conclusion: AMP proves to be a robust framework, and the paper demonstrates that sparsity, noise level, and regularization are key factors in determining the effectiveness of privacy-preserving methods for sparse high-dimensional models.

title: Incorporating contextual information into KGWAS for interpretable GWAS discovery
summary: Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS) identify associations between genetic variants and disease; however, moving beyond associations to causal mechanisms is critical for therapeutic target prioritization. The recently proposed Knowledge Graph GWAS (KGWAS) framework addresses this challenge by linking genetic variants to downstream gene-gene interactions via a knowledge graph (KG), thereby improving detection power and providing mechanistic insights. However, the original KGWAS implementation relies on a large general-purpose KG, which can introduce spurious correlations. We hypothesize that cell-type specific KGs from disease-relevant cell types will better support disease mechanism discovery. Here, we show that the general-purpose KG in KGWAS can be substantially pruned with no loss of statistical power on downstream tasks, and that performance further improves by incorporating gene-gene relationships derived from perturb-seq data. Importantly, using a sparse, context-specific KG from direct perturb-seq evidence yields more consistent and biologically robust disease-critical networks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes advancements to the KGWAS framework by using cell-type-specific knowledge graphs (KGs) for improved identification of causal mechanisms in Genome-Wide Association Studies.
AI.motivation: Understanding causal mechanisms of genetic variants beyond simple association is essential for prioritizing therapeutic targets.
AI.method: Enhance the KGWAS framework by pruning general-purpose KGs and utilizing cell-type-specific KGs derived from perturb-seq data.
AI.result: Cell-type-specific KGs boost performance in detecting disease-critical networks while maintaining statistical robustness.
AI.conclusion: Adopting sparse, context-specific KGs leads to biologically robust insights into disease mechanisms, advancing therapeutic research.

title: ArtHOI: Taming Foundation Models for Monocular 4D Reconstruction of Hand-Articulated-Object Interactions
summary: Existing hand-object interactions (HOI) methods are largely limited to rigid objects, while 4D reconstruction methods of articulated objects generally require pre-scanning the object or even multi-view videos. It remains an unexplored but significant challenge to reconstruct 4D human-articulated-object interactions from a single monocular RGB video. Fortunately, recent advancements in foundation models present a new opportunity to address this highly ill-posed problem. To this end, we introduce ArtHOI, an optimization-based framework that integrates and refines priors from multiple foundation models. Our key contribution is a suite of novel methodologies designed to resolve the inherent inaccuracies and physical unreality of these priors. In particular, we introduce an Adaptive Sampling Refinement (ASR) method to optimize object's metric scale and pose for grounding its normalized mesh in world space. Furthermore, we propose a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) guided hand-object alignment method, utilizing contact reasoning information as constraints of hand-object mesh composition optimization. To facilitate a comprehensive evaluation, we also contribute two new datasets, ArtHOI-RGBD and ArtHOI-Wild. Extensive experiments validate the robustness and effectiveness of our ArtHOI across diverse objects and interactions. Project: https://arthoi-reconstruction.github.io.

AI.tldr: ArtHOI introduces an optimization-based framework utilizing foundation models for reconstructing 4D human-articulated-object interactions from single monocular RGB videos, addressing inaccuracies and physical unrealities in prior methods.
AI.motivation: To tackle the challenge of 4D reconstruction of human-articulated-object interactions using only single-view RGB videos, overcoming limitations of rigid-object-focused HOI methods.
AI.method: ArtHOI refines priors from foundation models, introducing Adaptive Sampling Refinement for scaling and posing objects, and a Multimodal Large Language Model-guided hand-object alignment employing contact reasoning constraints.
AI.result: ArtHOI demonstrates robustness across various objects and human-object interaction scenarios, supported by extensive experiments and two new datasets, ArtHOI-RGBD and ArtHOI-Wild.
AI.conclusion: ArtHOI effectively addresses challenges in reconstructing 4D interactions involving humans and articulated objects, paving the way for advancements in this domain using foundation models.

title: Wattchmen: Watching the Wattchers -- High Fidelity, Flexible GPU Energy Modeling
comment: 16 pages, 14 figures. Accepted to the 2026 International Conference on Supercomputing (ICS '26)
summary: Modern GPU-rich HPC systems are increasingly becoming energy-constrained. Thus, understanding an application's energy consumption becomes essential. Unfortunately, current GPU energy attribution techniques are either inaccurate, inflexible, or outdated. Therefore, we propose Wattchmen, a flexible methodology for measuring, attributing, and predicting GPU energy consumption. We construct a per-instruction energy model using a diverse set of microbenchmarks to systematically quantify the energy consumption of GPU instructions, enabling finer-grain prediction and energy consumption breakdowns for applications. Compared with the state-of-the-art systems like AccelWattch (32%) and Guser (25%), across 16 popular GPGPU, graph analytics, HPC, and ML workloads, Wattchmen reduces the mean absolute percent error (MAPE) to 14% on V100 GPUs. Furthermore, we show that Wattchmen provides similar MAPEs for water-cooled V100s (15%) and extends to later architectures, including air-cooled A100 (11%) and H100 (12%) GPUs. Finally, to further demonstrate Wattchmen's value, we apply it to applications such as Backprop and QMCPACK, where Wattchmen's insights enable energy reductions of up to 35%.

AI.tldr: Wattchmen is a novel tool for fine-grained measurement, attribution, and prediction of GPU energy consumption, significantly reducing prediction errors and enabling energy optimizations in HPC and ML applications.
AI.motivation: Modern GPU-rich HPC systems face energy constraints, yet existing GPU energy attribution tools are inadequate due to inaccuracies or outdated methodologies.
AI.method: Wattchmen creates a detailed per-instruction energy model using diverse microbenchmarks, allowing fine-grain energy consumption analysis and prediction.
AI.result: Wattchmen improves GPU energy prediction accuracy, reducing mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) to 14% on V100 GPUs and showing similar accuracy across other GPU architectures.
AI.conclusion: Wattchmen offers a state-of-the-art solution for GPU energy attribution, enabling energy-efficient optimizations and demonstrating its capability across diverse workloads and architectures.

title: VolTune: A Fine-Grained Runtime Voltage Control Architecture for FPGA Systems
comment: It is a technical report for a design github repository that will be published with the current manuscript (https://github.com/CCIRT/VolTune/)
summary:
The rapid emergence of edge computing platforms and large-scale data centers has made power efficiency a primary design constraint, particularly for data-intensive and AI-driven workloads. Field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) are increasingly adopted due to their flexibility and potential for energy-efficient acceleration. However, FPGA supply voltages are typically fixed at design time using conservative margins, limiting the ability to adapt power consumption to runtime conditions.
  This paper presents VolTune, an open-source runtime voltage control architecture that enables runtime tuning of FPGA supply voltages through FPGA-integrated control logic that abstracts low-level PMBus operations. VolTune provides both hardware-based and software-based control paths, allowing designers to balance deterministic low-latency operation against programmability.
  In the presented prototype, the hardware-based control path achieves a measured end-to-end voltage transition latency of 2.3 ms, while the controller adds under 2% static power overhead and under 2% FPGA resource overhead. As a representative case study, VolTune is evaluated on the GTX transceiver supply rail of a Kintex-7 platform. The results show that runtime voltage tuning exposes a bounded operating region with clear trade-offs between energy efficiency and reliability, and achieves up to approximately 29.3% rail-power reduction at 10.0 Gbps when allowing BER up to 10e-6. These results show that FPGA-integrated runtime voltage control can provide practical energy savings with low integration overhead.

AI.tldr: VolTune introduces an open-source runtime voltage control for FPGAs, enabling energy-efficient voltage tuning with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: The rising need for power efficiency in edge computing and data centers, combined with the limitations of fixed FPGA supply voltages, motivated the development of an adaptive voltage tuning solution.
AI.method: Design and implementation of VolTune, which uses FPGA-integrated control logic for runtime voltage tuning, offering both hardware- and software-based control paths.
AI.result: VolTune achieves a 2.3 ms voltage transition latency, under 2% power and resource overhead, and demonstrates up to 29.3% power savings in a case study.
AI.conclusion: Runtime voltage tuning via VolTune provides practical energy-efficient solutions for FPGA-powered systems with low integration costs and manageable compromises in performance reliability trade-offs.

title: CADSmith: Multi-Agent CAD Generation with Programmatic Geometric Validation
summary: Existing methods for text-to-CAD generation either operate in a single pass with no geometric verification or rely on lossy visual feedback that cannot resolve dimensional errors. We present CADSmith, a multi-agent pipeline that generates CadQuery code from natural language. It then undergoes an iterative refinement process through two nested correction loops: an inner loop that resolves execution errors and an outer loop grounded in programmatic geometric validation. The outer loop combines exact measurements from the OpenCASCADE kernel (bounding box dimensions, volume, solid validity) with holistic visual assessment from an independent vision-language model Judge. This provides both the numerical precision and the high-level shape awareness needed to converge on the correct geometry. The system uses retrieval-augmented generation over API documentation rather than fine-tuning, maintaining a current database as the underlying CAD library evolves. We evaluate on a custom benchmark of 100 prompts in three difficulty tiers (T1 through T3) with three ablation configurations. Against a zero-shot baseline, CADSmith achieves a 100% execution rate (up from 95%), improves the median F1 score from 0.9707 to 0.9846, the median IoU from 0.8085 to 0.9629, and reduces the mean Chamfer Distance from 28.37 to 0.74, demonstrating that closed-loop refinement with programmatic geometric feedback substantially improves the quality and reliability of LLM-generated CAD models.

AI.tldr: CADSmith introduces a multi-agent pipeline for text-to-CAD generation, improving geometric precision and reliability via a dual-loop error correction and geometric validation process.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-CAD generation methods struggle with dimensional errors or lack geometric validation, necessitating a more robust and accurate approach.
AI.method: CADSmith employs iterative correction loops: an inner loop for execution errors and an outer loop for geometric validation using OpenCASCADE and visual feedback from a vision-language model. It uses retrieval-augmented generation rather than fine-tuning over a dynamic API database.
AI.result: CADSmith increases execution rates to 100% and significantly improves evaluation metrics: median F1 (0.9707→0.9846), IoU (0.8085→0.9629), and Chamfer Distance (28.37→0.74).
AI.conclusion: Incorporating closed-loop refinement with geometric feedback greatly enhances both the quality and reliability of CAD models generated from natural language.

title: Policy-Guided World Model Planning for Language-Conditioned Visual Navigation
summary: Navigating to a visually specified goal given natural language instructions remains a fundamental challenge in embodied AI. Existing approaches either rely on reactive policies that struggle with long-horizon planning, or employ world models that suffer from poor action initialization in high-dimensional spaces. We present PiJEPA, a two-stage framework that combines the strengths of learned navigation policies with latent world model planning for instruction-conditioned visual navigation. In the first stage, we finetune an Octo-based generalist policy, augmented with a frozen pretrained vision encoder (DINOv2 or V-JEPA-2), on the CAST navigation dataset to produce an informed action distribution conditioned on the current observation and language instruction. In the second stage, we use this policy-derived distribution to warm-start Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) planning over a separately trained JEPA world model, which predicts future latent states in the embedding space of the same frozen encoder. By initializing the MPPI sampling distribution from the policy prior rather than from an uninformed Gaussian, our planner converges faster to high-quality action sequences that reach the goal. We systematically study the effect of the vision encoder backbone, comparing DINOv2 and V-JEPA-2, across both the policy and world model components. Experiments on real-world navigation tasks demonstrate that PiJEPA significantly outperforms both standalone policy execution and uninformed world model planning, achieving improved goal-reaching accuracy and instruction-following fidelity.

AI.tldr: PiJEPA combines learned navigation policies and latent world model planning to improve instruction-conditioned visual navigation, outperforming standalone approaches on real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to solve limitations in embodied AI navigation concerning long-horizon planning and poor action initialization in high-dimensional spaces.
AI.method: The proposed framework has two stages: a policy is finetuned with a pretrained vision encoder to produce informed action distributions, and this policy-derived distribution initializes MPPI planning over a JEPA world model for improved prediction and sampling.
AI.result: PiJEPA surpasses standalone policy execution and uninformed planning in real-world navigation tasks, offering better goal accuracy and adherence to instructions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating informed policy priors with latent world model planning enhances instruction-conditioned navigation and demonstrates superior performance in challenging scenarios.

title: A Judge Agent Closes the Reliability Gap in AI-Generated Scientific Simulation
comment: 36 pages, 5 figures, 22 tables, includes Supplementary Information
summary: Large language models can generate scientific simulation code, but the generated code silently fails on most non-textbook problems. We show that classical mathematical validation -- well-posedness, convergence, and error certification -- can be fully automated by a Judge Agent, reducing the silent-failure rate from 42% to 1.5% across 134 test cases spanning 12 scientific domains. The headline result comes from a prospective benchmark: 72 blinded tasks submitted by 12 independent scientists yield an 89% success rate (95% CI: [80%, 95%]) with automated error bounds, versus 53% without the Judge. On clinical CT (the only powered experiment, n = 200), the pipeline reaches 99% of expert quality. The residual 1.5% concentrates at bifurcation points where certifiability breaks down. We formalize this boundary through the simulability class S and introduce spec.md, a structured specification format that makes any scientific computation problem machine-readable and solver-independent. Code, data, and all 72 benchmark tasks are publicly archived.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Judge Agent to automate mathematical validation of scientific simulation code generated by large language models, significantly reducing silent error rates.
AI.motivation: Large language models struggle with scientific simulation codes that silently fail in many complex scenarios, necessitating an automated way to validate and ensure their reliability.
AI.method: A Judge Agent automates classical mathematical validation (well-posedness, convergence, error certification), tested on 134 cases across 12 domains and a prospective benchmark on 72 tasks completed by independent scientists.
AI.result: The automated framework reduced silent-failure rates from 42% to 1.5%, achieving an 89% success rate in blinded benchmarks and 99% expert quality in clinical CT trials.
AI.conclusion: Automated mathematical validation through the Judge Agent is effective in improving the reliability of large language model-generated scientific codes, formalizing the process into the simulability class S and using structured specifications like spec.md.

title: Density-aware Soft Context Compression with Semi-Dynamic Compression Ratio
summary: Soft context compression reduces the computational workload of processing long contexts in LLMs by encoding long context into a smaller number of latent tokens. However, existing frameworks apply uniform compression ratios, failing to account for the extreme variance in natural language information density. While adopting a density-aware dynamic compression ratio seems intuitive, empirical investigations reveal that models struggle intrinsically with operations parameterized by input dependent, continuous structural hyperparameters. To resolve this pitfall, we introduce Semi-Dynamic Context Compression framework. Our approach features a Discrete Ratio Selector, which predicts a compression target based on intrinsic information density and quantizes it to a predefined set of discrete compression ratios. It is efficiently jointly trained with the compressor on synthetic data, with the summary lengths as a proxy to create labels for compression ratio prediction. Extensive evaluations confirm that our density-aware framework, utilizing mean pooling as the backbone, consistently outperforms static baselines, establishing a robust Pareto frontier for context compression techniques. Our code, data and model weights are available at https://github.com/yuyijiong/semi-dynamic-context-compress

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Semi-Dynamic Context Compression to improve computational efficiency in long context processing by using a discrete, density-aware compression approach.
AI.motivation: Existing compression methods for long contexts in LLMs fail to adjust to natural language's varying information density, leading to inefficiencies.
AI.method: A Semi-Dynamic Context Compression framework is proposed, featuring a Discrete Ratio Selector that predicts and adjusts compression ratios based on information density, trained with synthetic data.
AI.result: The framework outperforms static baselines in evaluations using mean pooling as a backbone, offering better efficiency and performance in context compression.
AI.conclusion: Semi-Dynamic Context Compression achieves a strong balance between efficiency and effectiveness, significantly advancing context compression techniques.

title: A Power-Weighted Noncentral Complex Gaussian Distribution
summary: The complex Gaussian distribution has been widely used as a fundamental spectral and noise model in signal processing and communication. However, its Gaussian structure often limits its ability to represent the diverse amplitude characteristics observed in individual source signals. On the other hand, many existing non-Gaussian amplitude distributions derived from hyperspherical models achieve good empirical fit due to their power-law structures, while they do not explicitly account for the complex-plane geometry inherent in complex-valued observations. In this paper, we propose a new probabilistic model for complex-valued random variables, which can be interpreted as a power-weighted noncentral complex Gaussian distribution.
